critique.summary: Scott Hanselman, a Microsoft employee, tweeted in March 2026 acknowledging dissatisfaction with Windows local accounts and stating that he is working on improving them. The tweet is brief and does not provide detailed information about the nature or timeline of the work.
mainPoints: Scott Hanselman expressed dislike for the current state of Windows local accounts.
He indicated that he is actively working on addressing issues related to Windows local accounts.
The statement was made in a single tweet without additional context or details.
critique.truthfulness: The claim is truthful as it is a direct statement from Scott Hanselman, a known Microsoft employee and developer advocate, on his verified Twitter account. However, the tweet provides limited information and does not specify what improvements or changes are being made, so the scope and impact of the work cannot be independently verified.
considerations: The tweet does not specify what problems exist with Windows local accounts or what solutions are being developed.
No official Microsoft announcement or detailed roadmap accompanies this statement, limiting the ability to assess the significance or timeline of the work.
The context of the tweet is limited to a personal expression and a brief update rather than a formal communication.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the challenges and skepticism around Windows local accounts, with users sharing frustrating experiences and doubts about the impact of Scott Hanselman's involvement. Some comments highlight poor software quality and question Microsoft's intentions and priorities.

comments.author: troad
comments.text:
Using Windows with a local account is a very weird experience. On the one hand, most of the adware and other nonsense is turned off. On the other hand, virtually nothing seems to have been tested against local accounts.

If I want to update my controller firmware, for example, I have to use the Xbox Accessories app, and it will try to open one or two Microsoft login windows on basically every click. They seem to take about 10 seconds to appear and load, during which time the app seems stuck. It actually works fine with a local account, but you're going to see about a dozen, slow-to-load login prompts, accompanying every step of getting your controller firmware updated. It's an insane experience. I can't imagine it's intentional, I assume the app is just desperately trying to connect to some Xbox overlay which presumes a Microsoft account and was never tested without one.

It just seems like it's part and parcel of Windows' generally abysmal software quality.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed, firsthand account of issues with local accounts on Windows, illustrating practical problems and supporting the claim of poor software quality.

comments.author: pndy
comments.text:
I expect this will be still buried down within sub-screens of OOBE. MS won't let this go so easily. They already captured enough users with MS accounts and such users won't simply be logged out and given local offline accounts.

For such users nothing will change and they'll be still exposed to all darkpatterns and shenanigans. This "kind" move is just for power-users.

Assuming of course this will actually happens and Hanselman won't be told "no".
comments.analysis: This comment offers a realistic perspective on Microsoft's business incentives and the probable limited scope of changes, reflecting an understanding of corporate strategy.

comments.author: kjellsbells
comments.text:
I don't know what "working on it" really means. Isn't Scott's job a sort of Azure/Windows developer relations gig? podcasts and speaking slots and Build demos. How is he working on it in any meaningful sense?

Not a knock on the guy, but it seems that fixing windows is way out of his wheelhouse and so the tweet has no value to me as a marker of any kind of tangible progress
comments.analysis: This comment critically evaluates the credibility of the announcement based on the author's role, providing a reasonable skepticism about the significance of the update.

comments.author: BrenBarn
comments.text: Working on something that already existed for decades?
comments.analysis: This comment appears dismissive and inaccurate, as the context suggests improvements or changes rather than reinventing local accounts.

comments.author: rkagerer
comments.text: For those of us not on X, is there some context, ie. the Tweet he's replying to?
comments.analysis: This comment is a neutral request for context, contributing little to the substantive discussion but helping clarify the conversation.

comments.author: jauntywundrkind
comments.text: Did Microsoft intentionally make NVMe slow for consumers, or was this a recent accident? It feels so user hostile to stick us with the decade and a half old really bad NVME-as-SCSI translation layer stack. And it seemed like the new stack was working well for users, only for Microsoft to take it away again. Why are you making your core experience so much worse, Microsoft? https://www.tomshardware.com/software/windows/microsoft-bloc...
comments.analysis: While relevant to Windows performance issues, this comment is off-topic from the local accounts discussion but raises valid concerns about Microsoft's handling of NVMe support.
